turns.text: Hello Cherri, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s strange sitting here, honestly. Usually, I’m the one holding the clipboard, standing at the foot of an isolette, trying to offer comfort to parents while my own mind is racing through differential diagnoses. But now, the silence in my house in Pembroke is just... heavy. When I’m at the hospital, the alarms and the hum of the machines are my comfort zone. Here, with just my daughter, I feel like a complete novice. 

People see my credentials and they think I’ve got this handled. They don't realize that my professional background is actually a bit of a curse. I look at her and I’m not just a mom; I’m a clinician constantly triaging. Is that breath a little too labored? Is her color perfect? I can’t turn off the part of my brain that’s been trained to look for the "what ifs." 

And then there's the appointments. The nurses and doctors, they treat me like one of them, using this professional shorthand. They skip the basic, gentle reassurances that I see them give other moms. I’m standing there, feeling like I’m drowning in this new reality—doing this on my own—and I’m just desperate for someone to look at me not as a colleague, but as a mom who needs to be told it’s going to be okay. It’s isolating. I just want to feel normal.
Tell me about the people who surrounded you in the days and weeks after your baby was born. Who showed up for you, and who were you surprised by — either by their presence or their absence?

That’s a complicated question for me. Because I’m a NICU nurse, I think people assumed I was the "super-mom" who didn’t need a village. My colleagues were kind, sure, but it felt like they checked a box—a quick text or a comment in the breakroom—and then moved on, assuming I knew how to handle the postpartum landscape because I’ve watched other women navigate it for years. 

There was a real sense of loneliness in that. I didn’t have a partner there to lean on, and I found myself waiting for someone to just show up at my door with a meal or to just sit with me so I wouldn’t have to be the "expert" for five minutes. 

It was surprising who actually stepped up. It wasn't the people I worked with, who I thought would get the "clinical" nature of the transition. It was my neighbor—someone I barely knew before this—who caught me crying while I was trying to get the car seat buckled one afternoon. She didn’t offer medical advice or talk shop; she just brought over dinner and asked how *I* was doing, not how the baby was doing. That lack of "professional" scrutiny was the most refreshing thing I’d felt in weeks. I realized then how much I’d been craving someone to just see me as a person, not a chart.

Tell me about the people involved in your feeding journey — whether you breastfed, formula-fed, or mixed-fed. Who helped you, who gave you conflicting advice, and how did those relationships shape your choices?

Feeding her... god, that was one of the areas where my background felt like the biggest hindrance. In the unit, we have strict protocols, measurements, and gold standards for how an infant *should* feed. When I started, I felt like I was constantly conducting a science experiment instead of just feeding my daughter.

I chose to breastfeed, but the pressure I put on myself to do it "perfectly"—based on all the textbooks I’ve practically memorized—was suffocating. The lactation consultants at the hospital were lovely, but they saw "Cherri the Nurse," not "Cherri the overwhelmed new mom." They’d give me technical jargon about latch mechanics and let-down reflex, and I’d just nod along, even when I was exhausted and my nipples were raw. They didn't stop to ask if I was okay, or if I needed a break.

The conflicting advice came from the most unexpected places—other nurses who had children would chime in with their "clinical" opinions on supplementation or timing. It made me feel like I was under a microscope. Eventually, I had to stop listening to the "experts" and just close the door. I started tuning out the clinical checklists and just focused on her hunger cues and my own intuition. It was a relief to finally stop treating feeding like a shift assignment and start treating it like a moment of connection. I’m still figuring it out, but it’s becoming less of a chart entry and more of a private, quiet space for us.
Who else was involved — a lactation consultant, a feeding group, an app, or family members — and how did their views align or conflict?

Honestly, I think I was the loudest voice in the room, and that was the problem. I relied heavily on a few tracking apps early on—the ones that let you log ounces, duration, and time intervals. It’s funny, isn't it? I spent my entire career tracking vitals, so I basically turned my own living room into a charting station. I was obsessed with the data. It made me feel like I had some control, even though the reality was that it just fueled my anxiety.

The lactation consultant at the hospital was professional, but there was this barrier. She’d look at my daughter’s weight gain chart and say, "You know the growth curves, Cherri, she’s right where she needs to be." It was meant to be reassuring, I’m sure, but it felt cold. It felt like a clinical update on a patient rather than a conversation between two women. She didn't acknowledge how hard it is to wake up every two hours when you’re doing it alone, or how emotionally draining it is when the baby won't settle.

My family, they were mostly hands-off. Since I’m a nurse, I think they felt like I had all the answers, so they didn't want to "meddle." They’d check in and ask, "Is everything by the book?" and I’d just lie and say yes. I didn't want to admit that even with all my training, I was terrified I was doing it wrong. I felt like I had to be the expert for everyone else, which meant I had no one to be the "clueless mom" with. It was just me, the baby, and the data, and none of that told me how to handle the loneliness.

Tell me about the people who noticed — or who missed — changes in your emotional wellbeing after your baby was born. Who checked in with you about how you were feeling as a person, not just as a new mother?

That’s the thing—almost nobody checked in on *me*. People would walk through my door and go straight for the baby. They’d ooh and ahh over her, ask how she was sleeping, or comment on how well she was gaining weight. I became a fixture in my own house, like a piece of furniture that was just there to facilitate the baby’s needs. 

Because I’m a nurse, there’s this unspoken assumption that I’m invincible. People see the "NICU nurse" label and assume I’m mentally bulletproof. My own mother, who I’m usually close with, would call and ask, "How’s the baby doing? Is she eating well?" and when I’d try to pivot to how lonely and hollow I felt, she’d just say, "Well, you know better than anyone that it's just a phase, you're the professional!" It felt like a door being slammed shut in my face.

I think the only person who really "noticed" was my sister, and even then, she only saw it because I finally broke down during a video call. I wasn't even talking about the baby; I was talking about how the quiet of the house was making me feel like I was losing my mind. She’s the only one who didn't try to "fix" it with a medical explanation or a piece of advice. She just stayed on the line with me, in silence, for twenty minutes. That was the first time in months I felt like I could actually breathe. Everyone else was looking for the nurse, but she was the only one looking for her sister.
Tell me about how your relationship with your partner — or your closest person — changed after your baby arrived. Who were you to each other, and how did the arrival of a baby reshape that?

Since I’m doing this on my own, I don't have a partner to navigate this with, which definitely changes the texture of everything. But I do have a "closest person"—my best friend, Sarah. She’s been in my life for years, and before the baby, we were the ones who went to brunch, talked about our career goals, and just enjoyed being two young women trying to figure out our thirties. 

After the baby arrived, that dynamic shifted into something I didn't expect. I think she’s a little intimidated by the "mom" thing. It’s like there’s this invisible barrier now. When she comes over, she’s so careful and quiet, almost like she’s visiting a hospital room. She’s worried about waking the baby or doing something "wrong" in front of the clinical expert, and I find myself retreating into that "nurse" persona just to make her feel comfortable. It’s exhausting. I miss being the person who could just be messy and unfiltered with her. 

Now, when we talk, the conversation always feels like it’s filtered through the lens of my motherhood. I crave the days where our biggest concern was where we were going on vacation, not my PPD anxiety or my struggles with sleep training. It’s lonely, feeling like the baby is the third wheel who has taken over our friendship. I’m still Cherri, I’m still the friend who loves a good laugh, but sometimes I feel like she’s afraid that if she pushes too hard, she’ll break something. I want her to just come over, put the baby in the bassinet, and tell me a story that has nothing to do with diapers or milestones. I need that connection back more than I can even put into words.
How did that affect your sense of who you were — as a partner, as an individual, as a new parent — in those early weeks and months?

It’s like my identity got completely eclipsed. Before, I was "Cherri," a person with a career, a social life, and a sense of self-assuredness that came from knowing my way around a hospital. Now? I’m just this vessel of vigilance. I feel like my personality has been hollowed out, replaced by this hyper-sensitive alarm system.

I’ve lost the ability to see myself as just an individual. When I’m alone with her, I don’t know who I am anymore because I’m so focused on being a "safe" environment for her. It’s a strange kind of ego death. I look in the mirror and I see the bags under my eyes, and I don't see the woman who used to go out for drinks with Sarah; I see a caregiver who is perpetually on the clock. 

I’ve started to resent the label of "new parent" because it feels like a demotion from being a person. I’m expected to be soft, domestic, and infinitely patient, but I’m still the same woman who spent years being tough and precise in the NICU. It’s hard to reconcile those two versions of myself. I feel like I’m wearing a costume that doesn't fit right. I’m mourning the woman I was, and I’m terrified that she’s never coming back—that I’ve been replaced by this anxious, lonely shadow. I just want to remember what it feels like to just *be* without having to be "responsible" for a life every single second of the day.

Tell me about the healthcare professionals who supported you after your birth — your community midwife, health visitor, GP. Which of those relationships felt continuous with your pregnancy care, and which felt like starting again from scratch?

Honestly, it felt like I was being handed off between strangers, even though I was within the same system. My OB, who I saw through the pregnancy, felt like a distant memory the moment I was discharged. There’s a specific "hand-off" in medicine, and once you’re postpartum, the focus shifts so abruptly that you feel like you’ve fallen through a crack.

When I started seeing the health visitor, it was jarring. They came in with their checklist—weighing, measuring, asking the standard questions—and it felt like starting completely from scratch. They didn't know the anxiety I had carried through the pregnancy, or the specific way I process stress as a clinician. They treated me like a blank slate, a "first-time mom," which felt patronizing given my background, yet simultaneously neglectful because they never once asked about *my* mental state. 

My GP was the worst of it. It felt like I was just another appointment slot to get through. I tried to hint that I was struggling—not with the baby’s health, but with my own head—and it was like they didn't have the bandwidth to listen. They were so focused on the baby’s progress that my own recovery seemed like an afterthought. I had this professional expectation that they would have some continuity of care, but it was just transactional. I felt like a patient file they hadn't bothered to read, rather than a woman in need of support. It made me realize that even when you know how the system works, it doesn't mean it’s going to work *for* you.

Tell me about your return to work — or your decision not to return — and the people involved in that transition. Who supported you, who put pressure on you, and how did that shape your experience of becoming a working parent?

The mere thought of returning to the unit makes my chest tighten. I’m currently on leave, but the ticking clock is always in the back of my mind. The pressure isn't necessarily coming from my manager—who is actually very accommodating—but from this crushing expectation I’ve built in my own head. My colleagues ask, "When are you coming back? We’re so short-staffed," and while they mean it as a compliment—that I’m needed and valued—it just sounds like a reminder that my value is tied to my labor, not my role as a mother.

There’s this weird duality. I miss the structure of the hospital, the clarity of a monitor, and the camaraderie of a team. But I’m terrified that if I go back, I’ll never be able to "turn off" the NICU mindset at home. If I spend twelve hours caring for other people’s babies, how much emotional bandwidth will I have left for my own? 

Some of the older nurses, the ones who raised kids while working night shifts, tell me it’s "manageable," as if that’s supposed to settle my nerves. But their version of "manageable" feels like a standard I’m not sure I can meet. I feel like I’m standing on the edge of a cliff, trying to decide whether to jump back into the world I know or stay here in this quiet, terrifying bubble. It’s a transition I feel completely unequipped for, despite having watched hundreds of other women do it. I just don't know if I can be the nurse and the mom at the same time without losing myself entirely in the middle.
How did that affect your sense of who you are — did going back to work feel like reclaiming something, or losing something, or both?

It feels like I’m standing at a crossroads where every path feels like a compromise. If I go back to the NICU, I’m reclaiming my identity as a professional—as "Cherri the Nurse"—which is a part of me that I miss desperately. I crave that sense of competence, that feeling of being good at something that isn't just about survival and diapers. I miss the shorthand of my coworkers and the satisfaction of a job where the metrics are clear and the outcomes, while sometimes heartbreaking, are part of a shared, practiced reality.

But then, the fear sets in. I feel like going back is also an admission that the "motherhood" piece is something I can’t handle on its own. I worry that if I step back into the hospital, I’m abandoning this fragile, quiet space I’ve been trying to cultivate with my daughter. Will I look at her and just see another patient? Will I come home so emotionally drained from saving other people's children that I have nothing left for my own? 

It’s both a reclaiming and a losing. I’m reclaiming my voice and my skills, but I’m losing that singular, uninterrupted (even if terrifying) focus on her. I’m terrified that I’ll be spread too thin, and that in trying to be both, I’ll fail at being either. It’s a transition that doesn't feel like a celebration of "having it all"; it feels like a high-stakes calculation where I’m not sure I can afford the price.
Tell me about the communities — online or in person — that became part of your postpartum world. Who did you connect with, and what did those communities offer that more formal support couldn't?

I honestly tried to avoid the online "mommy groups" at first. I knew too much about the medical misinformation that spreads in those spaces—the anecdotes disguised as clinical facts that make my skin crawl. But there came a night, around 3:00 AM, when the isolation was so deafening I finally gave in. I didn't go to the standard parenting forums, though. I found a small, niche group of other medical professionals—nurses, doctors, therapists—who had recently had babies.

That space was a godsend. It offered something the doctors and lactation consultants never could: radical, messy, unfiltered honesty. In that group, I didn't have to be the "expert." I could say, "I am a NICU nurse, and I am currently staring at my daughter’s chest rise and fall because I’m paralyzed with fear that she’ll stop breathing," and nobody told me I was being ridiculous or tried to reassure me with a growth chart. They just said, "Me too." 

They understood the duality of our brains—the way we can be incredibly capable in the hospital but feel completely incompetent in our own living rooms. It wasn't about "getting better" or "meeting milestones"; it was about witnessing each other’s humanity. It didn't replace formal care, but it filled the gaps that clinical, transactional appointments left wide open. It was the only place where I didn't feel like a medical chart—I finally felt like a person.
Tell me about any moments after your birth when you felt that you — as a person, not just as a new mother — were seen and cared for. Who made that happen, and what did they do or say?

I keep coming back to that afternoon with my neighbor. It sounds so small, but it was the first time in weeks that I wasn't being treated like a clinical problem to be solved or a nurse who "should know better." 

I was standing in the driveway, struggling with the infant seat, completely overwhelmed by the mechanics of it all—the click, the tension, the safety check. I was spiraling. When she walked over, she didn't ask about the baby’s weight or how many hours of sleep I was getting. She didn't look at me through that clinical lens. She just put a hand on my shoulder and said, "You look like you're carrying the weight of the world, Cherri. Come inside, I made too much lasagna, and we are just going to sit here until you're ready to breathe again."

She didn't offer to "fix" the car seat, and she didn't lecture me on baby safety. She just saw a woman who was fraying at the edges. She cleared the space for me to just exist as a person—not a NICU nurse, not a new mother, just a human being who was tired and lonely. That permission to be messy, to be vulnerable, and to be taken care of... it saved me. It made me realize that I’d been starving for that kind of human kindness, something that no medical protocol or hospital chart could ever provide.
Who else was involved in moments of genuine care for you — not just the baby — that stayed with you?

It’s hard for me to name many people, because the clinical armor I wear—or that people project onto me—is a thick barrier to reach through. But my sister, who lives a few towns over, has been the only one who consistently cuts through the noise.

One night, when I was spiraling about a minor cough—my NICU brain was practically convinced it was something catastrophic—she didn’t roll her eyes or tell me I was being an overprotective, paranoid nurse. She just asked me to turn on the video call and said, "Cherri, just look at me, not the monitor." She stayed on the line for nearly an hour, not talking about the baby’s vitals or feeding schedules, but telling me about a ridiculous mishap she’d had at work that day. She let me vent about the pressure of feeling like I have to know everything.

That was a moment of genuine care. She recognized that I was terrified and lonely, and instead of giving me a "professional" solution, she gave me companionship. She essentially validated that it’s okay to be a nurse who doesn't have all the answers for her own child. She reminded me that I’m allowed to be a sister and a friend, not just a clinician in the middle of a shift. It’s those moments, where someone chooses to see *me* instead of my qualifications, that actually stick. They’re the only things that make this transition feel survivable.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, I’ve been sitting here thinking about what I’d tell those nurses and OBs if I had the chance to step out of this "interview" and back into their offices. It’s this: stop looking at my credentials as a buffer. 

When you see a healthcare worker coming in for their own prenatal or postpartum care, don't assume we’re okay just because we understand the anatomy or the risks. Actually, it’s the opposite. We know *too much*. We’ve seen the worst-case scenarios, the rare complications, the stuff that never happens but you can't stop replaying in your head. 

I think the biggest thing for me is the need for someone to just hold space. I’m tired of being the person who knows the answer, or the person who’s expected to be strong because I wear scrubs. I wanted someone to sit me down and just say, "Cherri, you're the patient today. You don't have to be the nurse. Tell me how you're *feeling*—not what your vitals are." 

If I could change one thing about my care, it would be that—someone acknowledging that beneath the professional exterior, there’s a woman who is just as scared and just as fragile as any other new mom. I think that’s what I’d want the healthcare system to realize: being an expert doesn't mean you don't need to be cared for. It usually means you need it even more.
